title_en: The Prophecies of Muhammad in Scripture
title_ar: The Prophecies of Muhammad in Scripture
title_id: Nubuat Tentang Nabi Muhammad dalam Kitab Suci
content_id:
Pembahasan al-Kairanawi tentang nubuat-nubuat Alkitab mengenai Muhammad dalam Izhar al-Haqq termasuk yang paling rinci dalam tradisi apologetika Islam. Ia memeriksa serangkaian teks dari Perjanjian Lama maupun Baru yang secara tradisional diidentifikasi oleh para ulama Muslim sebagai ramalan kedatangan Muhammad, membahas argumen-argumen tekstual untuk interpretasi-interpretasi ini sekaligus respons-respons Kristen standar yang telah diajukan untuk menentangnya. Metodenya sepanjang karya ini adalah mengambil pertanyaan-pertanyaan tekstual dan linguistik dengan serius daripada sekadar menegaskan bacaan Islam, dan menunjukkan bahwa interpretasi Islam setidaknya sama-sama didukung oleh teks seperti alternatif Kristen, dan sering kali lebih baik didukung.

Pada Perjanjian Baru, al-Kairanawi mengembangkan secara panjang lebar argumen tentang bagian-bagian Paraklet dalam Injil Yohanes yang sebelumnya telah diperiksa oleh Ibnu Taimiyah. Ia terlibat lebih langsung dengan pertanyaan linguistik, mencatat bahwa manuskrip-manuskrip Yunani secara seragam memiliki parakletos daripada periklytos, tetapi berargumen bahwa keseragaman ini sendiri mungkin mencerminkan kerusakan sebuah teks yang awalnya membawa padanan dari bahasa Arab Ahmad. Ia juga berargumen bahwa bahkan mengambil teks yang diterima sebagai parakletos, deskripsi karakteristik Paraklet lebih alami cocok dengan sosok kenabian daripada Roh Kudus sebagaimana dipahami teologi Kristen dalam sosok itu. Paraklet yang 'tidak akan berbicara atas inisiatifnya sendiri' dan yang 'akan menceritakan hal-hal yang akan datang' cocok dengan profil seorang nabi yang menerima wahyu verbal dari Allah dan mengkomunikasikannya kepada umat manusia, yang merupakan deskripsi Islam tentang peran kenabian Muhammad.

Pada Perjanjian Lama, al-Kairanawi memeriksa teks Ibrani dari Kidung Agung, bab 5 ayat 16, yang menggunakan kata 'Muhammadim' atau 'Machamadim' sebagai deskripsi tentang kekasih. Dalam konteks puisi ini kata ini berfungsi sebagai kata benda umum yang berarti 'sangat indah' atau 'paling diinginkan,' tetapi al-Kairanawi mencatat bahwa akar M-H-M-D adalah padanan Ibrani yang tepat dari akar bahasa Arab nama Muhammad, yang berarti yang dipuji, yang sangat dianjurkan. Ia berargumen bahwa kehadiran nama ini dalam teks suci tidaklah kebetulan, dan bahwa terjemahan tradisional Yahudi dan Kristen sebagai kata sifat umum sendiri mungkin mencerminkan keputusan interpretatif untuk menghindari pengakuan terhadap ramalan tentang Muhammad. Ia juga memeriksa Ulangan 18:18, di mana Allah berjanji kepada Musa bahwa Ia akan membangkitkan seorang nabi 'seperti engkau dari antara saudara-saudaramu,' berargumen bahwa ini lebih alami cocok dengan Nabi Arab yang, seperti Musa, menerima syariat dan memimpin sebuah komunitas, daripada cocok dengan Isa, yang profil kenabiannya sangat berbeda dari Musa.

Al-Kairanawi mengakui bahwa argumen-argumen ini diperdebatkan dan bahwa para sarjana Kristen telah mengembangkan respons-respons untuk masing-masingnya. Tujuannya dalam Izhar al-Haqq bukan untuk mengklaim bahwa teks-teks Alkitab ini secara definitif membuktikan kenabian Muhammad kepada pembaca yang netral tetapi untuk menunjukkan bahwa interpretasi Islam terhadap teks-teks ini dapat dipertahankan, bahwa interpretasi itu didukung oleh pertimbangan-pertimbangan tekstual dan kontekstual, dan bahwa bacaan-bacaan alternatif Kristen tidak jelas lebih unggul. Kekuatan kumulatif dari beberapa teks yang menunjuk ke arah yang sama, ia berargumen, signifikan meskipun tidak ada teks tunggal yang merupakan argumen yang mematikan. Dikombinasikan dengan pernyataan Al-Qur'an bahwa Muhammad disebutkan dalam kitab suci terdahulu (7:157), argumen-argumen tekstual ini memberi pembaca Muslim dasar untuk keyakinan bahwa janji tentang nabi terakhir tertanam dalam bimbingan ilahi yang diberikan kepada komunitas-komunitas terdahulu, betapapun samarnya ia mungkin telah menjadi melalui transmisi dan interpretasi selanjutnya.
content_en:
Al-Kairanawi's treatment of biblical prophecies concerning Muhammad in Izhar al-Haqq is among the most detailed in the tradition of Islamic apologetics. He examines a series of texts from both the Old and New Testaments that Muslim scholars have traditionally identified as predictions of the coming of Muhammad, addressing both the textual arguments for these interpretations and the standard Christian responses that have been offered against them. His method throughout is to take the textual and linguistic questions seriously rather than simply asserting the Islamic reading, and to show that the Islamic interpretation is at least as well supported by the text as the Christian alternative, and often better supported.

On the New Testament, al-Kairanawi develops at length the argument about the Paraclete passages in the Gospel of John that Ibn Taymiyyah had earlier examined. He engages more directly with the linguistic question, noting that the Greek manuscripts uniformly have parakletos rather than periklytos, but arguing that this uniformity might itself reflect the corruption of a text that originally carried the cognate of the Arabic Ahmad. He also argues that even taking the received text as parakletos, the description of the Paraclete's characteristics matches a prophetic figure more naturally than the Holy Spirit as Christian theology has understood that figure. The Paraclete who 'will not speak on his own' and who 'will tell you of things to come' fits the profile of a prophet receiving verbal revelation from Allah and communicating it to humanity, which is precisely the Islamic description of Muhammad's prophetic role.

On the Old Testament, al-Kairanawi examines the Hebrew text of the Song of Songs, chapter 5 verse 16, which uses the word 'Muhammadim' or 'Machamadim' as a description of the beloved. In the context of the poem this word functions as a common noun meaning 'altogether lovely' or 'most desirable,' but al-Kairanawi notes that the root M-H-M-D is the exact Hebrew equivalent of the Arabic root of Muhammad's name, meaning the praised one, the highly commended one. He argues that the presence of this name in the sacred text is not accidental, and that the traditional Jewish and Christian translation as a common adjective may itself reflect an interpretive decision to avoid acknowledging a prediction of Muhammad. He also examines Deuteronomy 18:18, where Allah promises Moses that He will raise up a prophet 'like you from among their brothers,' arguing that this fits the Arabian Prophet who, like Moses, received a law and led a community, more naturally than it fits Jesus, whose prophetic profile is quite different from Moses's.

Al-Kairanawi acknowledges that these arguments are contested and that Christian scholars have developed responses to each of them. His goal in Izhar al-Haqq is not to claim that these biblical texts conclusively prove Muhammad's prophethood to a neutral reader but rather to show that the Islamic interpretation of these texts is defensible, that it is supported by textual and contextual considerations, and that the Christian alternative readings are not obviously superior. The cumulative force of multiple texts pointing in the same direction, he argues, is significant even if no single text is a knock-down argument. Combined with the Quranic assertion that Muhammad is mentioned in the earlier scriptures (7:157), these textual arguments give the Muslim reader grounds for confidence that the promise of the final prophet was embedded in the divine guidance given to earlier communities, however obscured it may have become through subsequent transmission and interpretation.
content_ar: Al-Kairanawi's treatment of biblical prophecies concerning Muhammad in Izhar al-Haqq is among the most detailed in the tradition of الإسلامي apologetics. He examines a series of texts from both the Old and New Testaments that المسلم العلماء have traditionally identified as predictions of the coming of Muhammad, addressing both the textual arguments for these interpretations and the standard Christian responses that have been offered against them. His method throughout is to take the textual and linguistic questions seriously rather than simply asserting the الإسلامي reading, and to show that the الإسلامي interpretation is at least as well supported by the text as the Christian alternative, and often better supported. On the New Testament, al-Kairanawi develops at length the argument about the Paraclete passages in the Gospel of John that ابن تيمية had earlier examined. He engages more directly with the linguistic question, noting that the Greek manuscripts uniformly have parakletos rather than periklytos, but arguing that this uniformity might itself reflect the corruption of a text that originally carried the cognate of the Arabic Ahmad. He also argues that even taking the received text as parakletos, the description of the Paraclete's characteristics matches a prophetic figure more naturally than the Holy Spirit as Christian theology has understood that figure. The Paraclete who 'will not speak on his own' and who 'will tell you of things to come' fits the profile of a prophet receiving verbal revelation from الله and communicating it to humanity, which is precisely the الإسلامي description of Muhammad's prophetic role. On the Old Testament, al-Kairanawi examines the Hebrew text of the الغناء of الغناء, chapter 5 verse 16, which uses the word 'Muhammadim' or 'Machamadim' as a description of the beloved. In the context of the poem this word functions as a common noun meaning 'altogether lovely' or 'most مستحب,' but al-Kairanawi notes that the root M-H-M-D is the exact Hebrew equivalent of the Arabic root of Muhammad's name, meaning the praised one, the highly commended one. He argues that the presence of this name in the sacred text is not accidental, and that the traditional Jewish and Christian translation as a common adjective may itself reflect an interpretive decision to avoid acknowledging a prediction of Muhammad. He also examines Deuteronomy 18:18, where الله promises Moses that He will raise up a prophet 'like you from among their brothers,' arguing that this fits the Arabian Prophet who, like Moses, received a law and led a community, more naturally than it fits Jesus, whose prophetic profile is quite different from Moses's. Al-Kairanawi acknowledges that these arguments are contested and that Christian العلماء have developed responses to each of them. His goal in Izhar al-Haqq is not to claim that these biblical texts conclusively prove Muhammad's prophethood to a neutral reader but rather to show that the الإسلامي interpretation of these texts is defensible, that it is supported by textual and contextual considerations, and that the Christian alternative readings are not obviously superior. The cumulative force of multiple texts pointing in the same direction, he argues, is significant even if no single text is a knock-down argument. Combined with the Quranic assertion that Muhammad is mentioned in the earlier scriptures (7:157), these textual arguments give the المسلم reader grounds for confidence that the promise of the final prophet was embedded in the divine guidance given to earlier communities, however obscured it may have become through subsequent transmission and interpretation.
